title: Silent Spring
rationale: Silent Spring stands as the foundational text of the modern global environmental movement. First published 1962 by Houghton Mifflin; this edition is the 2002 Mariner Books/Houghton Mifflin reprint with an introduction by Linda Lear and an afterword by Edward O. Wilson. The book documented with rigorous scientific evidence how synthetic pesticides — DDT, dieldrin, heptachlor, and others — bioaccumulate through food chains, cause ecological collapse and reproductive failure in wildlife, and pose documented carcinogenic and neurological risks to humans. Its 17 chapters move from prophetic fable through chemical catalogues and field case studies of water, soil, plants, birds, and fish to its urgent health chapters on human cancer and poisoning. Rachel Carson was a marine biologist, government scientist, and already celebrated nature writer when she produced this work at the cost of her life (she died 1964). The book directly precipitated the U.S. EPA's DDT ban (1972), the creation of the EPA (1970), and a worldwide environmental consciousness that persists today.
verifiedVia: Open Library (OL3670355M), Google Books, WorldCat, rachelcarson.org
